sections.label: A Lakh Varieties!
sections.html:
• Defining a Lakh: 1,00,000 (One Lakh) is the smallest 6-digit number.

  • The Pattern:

    • Largest 3-digit number (999) + 1 = 1,000 (Smallest 4-digit).

    • Largest 4-digit number (9,999) + 1 = 10,000 (Smallest 5-digit).

    • Largest 5-digit number (99,999) + 1 = 1,00,000 (Smallest 6-digit).

  • Visualizing the Size: 1 lakh days is roughly 274 years.

    • 1 lakh people standing shoulder-to-shoulder would stretch 38 kilometers.

    • The human head has about 1 lakh hairs.

sections.label: Land of Tens
sections.html:
• Conversions:

    • {tex}100 \text{ tens} = 1,000{/tex} (One thousand)

    • {tex}100 \text{ thousands} = 1,00,000{/tex} (One lakh)

    • {tex}1,000 \text{ hundreds} = 1,00,000{/tex} (One lakh)

  • Creative Expressions: Numbers can be broken down in multiple ways (e.g., 5072 can be 50 hundreds + 7 tens + 2 ones OR 5 thousands + 72 ones).

sections.label: Of Crores and Crores!
sections.html:
Comparison between the naming and comma systems used in India and internationally.

Indian System International (American) System Numbers of Zeroes
1 Lakh 100 Thousand 5
10 Lakhs 1 Million 6
1 Crore 10 Million 7
10 Crores 100 Million 8
1 Arab (100 Crores) 1 Billion 9

Comma Pattern:

  • Indian: 3-2-2-2 (e.g., 9,87,65,01,234)

  • International: 3-3-3-3 (e.g., 9,876,501,234)

sections.label: Exact and Approximate Values
sections.html:
In real life, we often "round off" large numbers because the exact digit isn't always necessary.

  • Rounding Up: Used for safety/buffer (e.g., ordering 750 sweets for 732 people).

  • Rounding Down: Often used in casual pricing or estimates (e.g., ₹470 called "around ₹450").

  • Nearest Neighbors: Identifying the closest thousand, lakh, or crore to simplify data.

sections.label: Patterns in Products
sections.html:
Using algebra-like logic to solve large multiplications quickly.

  • Multiplying by 5: Multiply by 10 and divide by 2 ({tex}116 \times 5 \rightarrow 1160 \div 2 = 580{/tex}).

  • Multiplying by 25: Multiply by 100 and divide by 4 ({tex}824 \times 25 \rightarrow 82400 \div 4 = 20600{/tex}).

  • Predicting Digits:  The product of two 2-digit numbers is always either a 3-digit or 4-digit number.

    • Logic: {tex}10 \times 10 = 100{/tex} (Smallest) and {tex}100 \times 100 = 10,000{/tex} (so {tex}99 \times 99{/tex} must be less than 10,000).

sections.label: Did You Ever Wonder…?
sections.html:
• Stage-wise Calculation: To see if the population of Mumbai fits in 1 lakh buses:

    1. Assume 1 bus = 50 people.

    2. 1 lakh buses = 50 lakh people.

    3. Mumbai population > 1.24 crore (124 lakhs).

    4. Result: No, they will not fit.

  • Distance/Time Estimates: Calculating if traveling 100 km/day for 10 years reaches the moon (3,84,400 km).

    • {tex}100 \times 365 \times 10 = 3,65,000 \text{ km}{/tex}. (Close, but not quite!)

sections.label: Simple Expressions
sections.html:
• Definition: An arithmetic expression is a mathematical "phrase" using numbers and operations (e.g., {tex}13 + 2{/tex}, {tex}12 \times 5{/tex}).

  • The Value: Every expression evaluates to a single number, called its value.

    • Example: The value of {tex}18 \div 3{/tex} is {tex}6{/tex}.

  • Equality: We use the {tex}={/tex} sign to show that an expression and its value are the same.

    • {tex}10 + 2 = 12{/tex}.

  • Comparison: Expressions can be compared using {tex}>{/tex} (greater than) or {tex}<{/tex} (less than) based on their final values.

    • {tex}10 + 2 > 7 + 1{/tex} (because {tex}12 > 8{/tex}).

Instead of calculating large numbers, you can compare expressions by looking at the relationship between the numbers.

  • Addition Comparison: 1023+125 vs. 1022+128.

    • The first number in the first expression is 1 more than the first number in the second.

    • The second number in the first expression is 3 less than the second number in the second.

    • Net result: The second expression is greater by 2.

  • Subtraction Comparison: 113−25=112−24.

    • The starting amount is 1 higher, but the amount removed is also 1 higher. They balance out.

  • Balancing: In 13 + 4 = ________+ 6, we see the left side is {tex}17{/tex}. To make the right side {tex}17{/tex}, the blank must be {tex}11{/tex}.

  • Targeting: Using different operations to reach a goal. To reach {tex}12{/tex}:

    • Addition: {tex}10 + 2{/tex}

    • Multiplication: {tex}3 \times 4{/tex}

    • Division: {tex}24 \div 2{/tex}

sections.label: Reading and Evaluating Complex Expressions
sections.html:
• The Problem: {tex}30 + 5 \times 4{/tex} can be confusing. Is it {tex}35 \times 4 = 140{/tex} or {tex}30 + 20 = 50?{/tex}  

  • Brackets: Brackets {tex}( ){/tex} are punctuation for math. Always solve what is inside the brackets first.

    • {tex}100 - (15 + 56) = 100 - 71 = 29{/tex}.

  • Terms: Terms are parts of an expression separated by a {tex}+{/tex} sign.

    • In {tex}6 \times 5 + 3{/tex}, the terms are {tex}(6 \times 5){/tex} and {tex}3{/tex}.

    • Rule: Evaluate the multiplication/division terms before adding them together.

  • Distributive Property: Multiplying a sum is the same as the sum of the multiples.

    • {tex}2 \times (43 + 24) = (2 \times 43) + (2 \times 24){/tex}.

    • {tex}(4 + 3) \times 5 = (4 \times 5) + (3 \times 5){/tex}.

  • Tinkering for Speed: Use the distributive property to solve hard multiplication in your head.

    • To solve {tex}97 \times 25{/tex}: Think of it as {tex}(100 - 3) \times 25{/tex}.

    • {tex}= (100 \times 25) - (3 \times 25){/tex}

    • {tex}= 2500 - 75 = 2425{/tex}.

  • Logical Adjustments: If you know {tex}53 \times 18 = 954{/tex}, you can find {tex}63 \times 18{/tex} by just adding 10 more 18s ({tex}180{/tex}) to your original answer.

sections.label: The Need for Smaller Units
sections.html:
When we measure objects, sometimes whole units like 1 cm are not enough to give an exact length. For example, two screws may look the same but differ slightly in length. To measure such small differences, we divide 1 cm into 10 equal parts. Each part is called {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} cm.

So, a length like {tex}2 \frac{7}{10} {~cm}{/tex} means:

{tex} 2+\frac{7}{10}=2.7 {~cm} {/tex}

This helps us measure more accurately.
Thus, smaller units are needed when precise measurement is required. Without dividing units, we cannot measure tiny differences correctly. This idea is used in rulers, scales, and scientific measurements where exact values are important.

sections.label: A Tenth Part
sections.html:
When one unit is divided into 10 equal parts, each part is called one-tenth:

{tex} \frac{1}{10} {/tex}

So, {tex}3 \frac{4}{10}{/tex} means:

{tex} 3+\frac{4}{10}=\frac{34}{10} {/tex}

Also,

{tex} 10 \times \frac{1}{10}=1 {/tex}

This means 10 tenths make one whole.
Numbers can be written in two ways:

{tex} 4 \frac{1}{10}=\frac{41}{10} {/tex}

This shows that fractional parts can be combined into improper fractions. Understanding tenths helps in measuring, comparing, and adding quantities easily. It is the first step towards understanding decimals and place value beyond whole numbers.

sections.label: A Hundredth Part
sections.html:
To measure even more precisely, each tenth is divided into 10 equal parts. So:

{tex} 1 \text { unit }=100 \text { hundredths } {/tex}

Each part is:

{tex} \frac{1}{100} {/tex}

For example:

{tex} 4 \frac{4}{10} \frac{5}{100}=4+\frac{4}{10}+\frac{5}{100} {/tex}

Also,

{tex} 1 \operatorname{tenth}=\frac{10}{100} {/tex}

This means 10 hundredths make 1 tenth.
Hundredths allow us to measure very small differences. For example, paper thickness or small lengths can be expressed in hundredths. This improves accuracy in measurement and prepares us for decimal numbers like 4.45.

sections.label: Decimal Place Value
sections.html:
Decimal Place Value
The decimal system is based on powers of 10 . Just like whole numbers have place values (ones, tens, hundreds), decimals extend this system to smaller parts.

Place values:

{tex} \text { Hundreds } \rightarrow \text { Tens } \rightarrow \text { Ones } \rightarrow \frac{1}{10} \rightarrow \frac{1}{100} {/tex}

Example:

{tex} 70.5=7 \times 10+5 \times \frac{1}{10} {/tex}
{tex} 7.05=7 \times 1+5 \times \frac{1}{100} {/tex}

The decimal point separates whole numbers from fractional parts.
This system helps us clearly represent numbers like 7.05 and 70.5, which are different. Decimal place value is important for reading, writing, and understanding numbers accurately.

sections.label: Units of Measurement
sections.html:
Units of Measurement

Measurement units are related using powers of 10 .
Length:

{tex} 1 {~cm}=10 {~mm}, 1 {~mm}=0.1 {~cm} {/tex}
{tex} 1 {~m}=100 {~cm}, 1 {~cm}=0.01 {~m} {/tex}

Example:

{tex} 5.6 {~cm}=56 {~mm} {/tex}

Weight:

{tex} 1 {~kg}=1000 {~g}, 1 {~g}=0.001 {~kg} {/tex}

Money:

{tex} 1 \text { rupee }=100 \text { paise }, 75 \text { paise }=0.75 \text { rupee } {/tex}

These conversions help us express quantities in different units. Decimal form makes calculations easier and more systematic in daily life and science.

sections.label: Locating and Comparing Decimals
sections.html:
Decimals lie between whole numbers.
For example:
1.4 lies between 1 and 2

On a number line, the space between 1 and 2 is divided into 10 equal parts.
To compare decimals:
Compare digits from left to right. Example:

{tex} 6.456 \text { and } 6.465 {/tex}

Units and tenths are same, but:

{tex} 5<6 \text { (in hundredths place) } {/tex} So,

{tex} 6.456<6.465 {/tex}

Also:

{tex} 0.2=0.20=0.200 {/tex}

But

{tex} 0.2 \neq 0.02 {/tex}

Thus, place value helps us compare decimals correctly and understand their exact position.

sections.label: More on the Decimal System
sections.html:
Decimals are added and subtracted like whole numbers, keeping the decimal points aligned.

Example (Addition):

{tex} 2.7+3.5=6.2 {/tex}

Example (Subtraction):

{tex} 3.5-2.7=0.8 {/tex}

In fractional form:

{tex} 2 \frac{7}{10}+3 \frac{5}{10}=6 \frac{2}{10} {/tex}

If needed, we regroup:

{tex} 5 \frac{13}{10}=6 \frac{3}{10} {/tex}

For hundredths:

{tex} 15 \frac{34}{100}+2 \frac{68}{100}=18 \frac{2}{100} {/tex}

sections.label: The Notion of Letter-Numbers
sections.html:
• There are different branches of mathematics. One of them is arithmetic, in which we apply the knowledge of the propcrties of numbers and operations on numbers in solving our day-to-day problems. There is one more branch of mathematics, called algebra, in which letter-numbers are used for unknown quantities. numbers, arithmetic operations and rules for manipulating those symbals.
  • The main feature of algebra is that we use letter-numbers like x, y, z, a. b, c, …, etc. These letters are used in place of numbers, to represent any number or unknown quantity. This helps us to write rules and formulae in a general way.

  • Like arithmetical expression, expressions formed by using letters, numbers and operation signs (+, —, ×, ÷) are called algebraic expressions.

  • Letters such as a, b, c, … , x, y, z, etc., that arc used to represent numbers arc called Letter-numbers. Letter-numbers as well as numerals arc used in algebraic expressions.

  • The value of a letter-number is not fixed. We use letter-numbers to present an unknown number or quantity and denote it by any letter such as 1, m, n, p, x, y, z etc.
  • Algebraic expressions use the same rules as arithmetic expressions.
  • Both algebraic and non-algebraic expressions can be explained in everyday language, as they often represent patterns or connections that are simple to describe.
  • Formulas employ algebraic expressions to model patterns and mathematical relationships between quantities as well as to provide predictions.
  • A variable allows us to express relations in any practical situation and to express many common rules and properties of geometry, algebra, etc.
  • Algebraic expressions are used in formulas to model patterns and mathematical relationships between quantities, and to make predictions.
  • Algebraic expressions use not only numbers but also letter-numbers.
  • The rules for manipulating arithmetic expressions also apply to algebraic expressions. These rules can be used to reduce algebraic expressions to their simplest forms.
  • Algebraic expressions can be described in ordinary language, and vice versa. Patterns or relationships that are easily written using algebra can often be long and complex in ordinary language. This is one of the advantages of algebra.

sections.label: Revisiting Arithmetic Expressions
sections.html:
• Before mastering algebra, we must be experts at arithmetic expressions (which use only numbers). We have learned that expressions are made of terms added together. For example, in {tex}23 - 10 \times 2{/tex}, the terms are {tex}23{/tex} and {tex}(-10 \times 2){/tex}.
  • We evaluate multiplication before addition. So, {tex}23 + (-20) = 3{/tex}. We also use swapping (Commutative Property: {tex}a + b = b + a{/tex}) and grouping (Associative Property) to make calculation easier. For instance, {tex}83 + 28 - 13 + 32{/tex} is easier if we group {tex}(83 - 13) + (28 + 32) = 70 + 60 = 130{/tex}. When a negative sign is outside a bracket, like {tex}68 - (18 + 13){/tex}, it applies to everything inside: {tex}68 - 18 - 13 = 37{/tex}. Algebraic expressions follow these exact same rules when we replace letters with numbers.

sections.label: Omission of the Multiplication Symbol in Algebraic Expressions
sections.html:
• In algebra, we like to be concise. When a number and a letter-number are multiplied, we usually skip the multiplication sign ({tex}\times{/tex}). For example, {tex}4 \times n{/tex} is simply written as {tex}4n{/tex}. This is very common in sequences. In the sequence {tex}4, 8, 12, 16 \dots{/tex}, every term is a multiple of 4. The {tex}n^{\text{th}}{/tex} term is {tex}4 \times n{/tex}, written as {tex}4n{/tex}.
  • If an expression is {tex}5m + 3{/tex}, and we are told {tex}m = 2{/tex}, we calculate it as {tex}(5 \times 2) + 3 = 13{/tex}.
    Note of caution: {tex}4n{/tex} does NOT mean the digits are placed side-by-side like {tex}42{/tex}. If {tex}n=2{/tex}, {tex}4n{/tex} is {tex}8{/tex}, not {tex}42{/tex}. This shorthand makes long expressions like {tex}2l + 2b{/tex} (the perimeter of a rectangle) much cleaner and easier to read than {tex}2 \times l + 2 \times b{/tex}.

sections.label: Simplification of Algebraic Expressions
sections.html:
• Simplifying means making an expression as short as possible by combining Like Terms. Like terms are terms that have the exact same letter-numbers. For example, {tex}5c, 3c,{/tex} and {tex}10c{/tex} are like terms because they all use '{tex}c{/tex}'. We can add them using the Distributive Property: {tex}5c + 3c + 10c = (5 + 3 + 10)c = 18c{/tex}.
  • However, Unlike Terms cannot be combined. In the expression {tex}18c + 11d{/tex} (cost of pencils and erasers), we cannot simplify further because {tex}c{/tex} and {tex}d{/tex} represent different things. When simplifying, we also use brackets. For example, {tex}4(x + y) - y{/tex} expands to {tex}4x + 4y - y{/tex}. Since {tex}4y{/tex} and {tex}-y{/tex} are like terms, it simplifies to {tex}4x + 3y{/tex}. This process of "cleaning up" expressions helps us solve complex problems with fewer steps.

sections.label: Pick Patterns and Reveal Relationships
sections.html:
• Algebra is a powerful tool for describing patterns in the world around us, such as in calendars or matchstick designs. Consider a matchstick pattern forming triangles: 1 triangle uses 3 sticks, 2 triangles use 5 sticks, and 3 triangles use 7 sticks. We notice that for every new triangle, we add 2 more sticks.
  • We can reveal this relationship with a formula. Since we start with 3 and add 2 for every step after the first, the formula for step {tex}y{/tex} is {tex}3 + 2(y - 1){/tex}. If we simplify this: {tex}3 + 2y - 2 = 2y + 1{/tex}. Using this formula, we can instantly find the sticks needed for Step 100 ({tex}2 \times 100 + 1 = 201{/tex}) without drawing it! Algebra allows us to prove that patterns—like diagonal sums in a calendar being equal—are true for any number, not just the ones we can see.

sections.label: Across the Line
sections.html:
Imagine taking a square piece of paper and folding it in different ways.

  • If you draw lines along the creases, you'll notice various lines on the paper. What happens if you look at any two of these lines? Do they cross each other? If they don't cross on the paper, would they meet if you could extend them infinitely?
  • When two lines meet or cross each other at a single point on a flat surface, we say they intersect. The point where they cross is called the point of intersection.

Let's look closer at what happens when two lines intersect. Consider line I intersecting line m as shown below:

How many angles are formed at the point of intersection?
We can see four angles, labeled {tex}{a}, {b}, {c}{/tex}, and d.

Thinking about the Angles:
Look back at Figure above. If we know the measure of one angle, say {tex}\angle {a}=120^{\circ}{/tex}, can we figure out the measures of the other angles without measuring?
- Angles {tex}\angle {a}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} form a straight line together. A straight angle measures {tex}{1 8 0}^{\circ}{/tex}. So, {tex}\angle {a}+ \angle {b}=180^{\circ}{/tex}. If {tex}\angle {a}=120^{\circ}{/tex}, then {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} must be {tex}180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}{/tex}.
- Similarly, {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {c}{/tex} also form a straight line. So, {tex}\angle {b}+\angle {c}=180^{\circ}{/tex}. If {tex}\angle {b}=60^{\circ}{/tex}, then {tex}\angle {c}{/tex} must be {tex}180^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=120^{\circ}{/tex}.
- And, {tex}\angle {c}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {d}{/tex} form a straight line. So, {tex}\angle {c}+\angle {d}=180^{\circ}{/tex}. If {tex}\angle {c}=120^{\circ}{/tex}, then {tex}\angle {d}{/tex} must be {tex}180^{\circ}-120^{\circ}=60^{\circ}{/tex}.

Notice something interesting? {tex}\angle {a}=\angle {c}=120^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {b}=\angle {d}=60^{\circ}{/tex}.
The angles opposite each other at the intersection point are equal!
Linear Pair: Adjacent angles formed by two intersecting lines that add up to {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} (like {tex}\angle{/tex} a and {tex}\angle {b}{/tex}, or {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {c}{/tex} ). They form a straight line together.Vertically Opposite Angles: Angles that are opposite each other when two lines intersect (like {tex}\angle {a}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {c}{/tex}, or {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {d}{/tex} ). Vertically opposite angles are always equal to each other. This is a fundamental concept, a proof in mathematics!
 

Perpendicular LInes: If all four angles are equal and they form a complete circle (360o), what would each angle measure?

That's right, each angle would be {tex}360^{\circ} / 4=90^{\circ}{/tex}.
 A pair of lines that intersect each other at a perfect right angle {tex}\left(90^{\circ}\right){/tex}.
We use a small square symbol at the intersection to show that lines are perpendicular.
In the figure above, lines {tex}I{/tex} and {tex}m{/tex} are perpendicular to each other.

sections.label: Between Lines
sections.html:
Let's look at different ways lines can relate to each other. Observe the line segments in the figure below.

Are line segments ST and UV likely to meet if they are extended?
Ans: If the lines are not parallel, they will likely intersect at some point. Thus, line segments S and UV are likely to meet when extended, as they are not parallel.

Are line segments OP and QR likely to meet if they are extended?
Ans: If two lines are parallel, they will never meet, regardless of how far they are extended. Thus, line segments OP and QR didn't meet when extended, as they are parallel.

Here are some examples of lines we notice around us.

These lines seem to run alongside each other, always keeping the same distance apart, and never intersecting. These are called parallel lines.

Parallel Lines: A pair of lines on the same flat surface (plane) that never meet or intersect, no matter how far they are extended in either direction.

Can you spot some parallel lines in your classroom or home?

sections.label: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Paper  Folding
sections.html:
Notations
How do we show lines are parallel or perpendicular in diagrams without writing it out every time?
- Parallel Lines: We use arrow marks (>) on the lines. If there's more than one set of parallel lines, we use double arrows (>>), then triple arrows (>>>), and so on, for each different set.
- Perpendicular Lines: We draw a small square symbol at the point of intersection where the right angle ( {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} ) is formed.


Representation of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

sections.label: Transversals
sections.html:
Transversal: A line that intersects (crosses) two or more other lines (which may or may not be parallel) at distinct points.

 

Types of Angles Formed by a Transversal:
1. Interior Angles: Angles that lie between the two lines I and m. (In Fig: {tex}\angle 3, \angle 4, \angle 5, \angle 6{/tex} )
2. Exterior Angles: Angles that lie outside the two lines I and m. (In Fig: {tex}\angle 1, \angle 2, \angle 7, \angle 8{/tex} )
3. Pairs of Corresponding Angles: Angles that are in the same relative position at each intersection point. Think of them as being in the "same corner". (Pairs: {tex}(\angle 1, \angle 5),(\angle 2, \angle 6),(\angle 3, \angle 7),(\angle 4, \angle 8)){/tex}
4. Pairs of Alternate Interior Angles: Angles that are between the two lines but on opposite sides of the transversal. (Pairs: {tex}(\angle 3, \angle 5),(\angle 4, \angle 6)){/tex}
5. Pairs of Alternate Exterior Angles: Angles that are outside the two lines and on opposite sides of the transversal. (Pairs: {tex}(\angle 2, \angle 8),(\angle 1, \angle 7){/tex} )
6. Pairs of Interior Angles on the Same Side of the Transversal: Angles that are between the two lines and on the same side of the transversal. (Pairs: {tex}(\angle 3, \angle 5),(\angle 4, \angle 6){/tex}). These are sometimes called consecutive interior angles or co-interior angles.

 

Corresponding Angles:

1. Corresponding Angles are Equal: If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of corresponding angles is equal. (e.g., {tex}\angle 1=\angle 5, \angle 2=\angle 6, \angle 3=\angle 7, \angle 4=\angle 8{/tex} )
- Converse is also true: If a transversal intersects two lines such that a pair of corresponding angles is equal, then the two lines are parallel.
2. Alternate Interior Angles are Equal: If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of alternate interior angles is equal. (e.g., {tex}\angle 3=\angle 6, \angle 4=\angle 5{/tex} )
- Converse is also true: If a transversal intersects two lines such that a pair of alternate interior angles is equal, then the two lines are parallel.
3. Interior Angles on the Same Side are Supplementary: If a transversal intersects two parallel lines, then each pair of interior angles on the same side of the transversal is supplementary (they add up to {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} ). (e.g., {tex}\angle 3+\angle 5=180^{\circ}, \angle 4+\angle 6=180^{\circ}{/tex} )

sections.label: Drawing Parallel Lines
sections.html:
Drawing Parallel Lines
Parallel lines are two lines that never meet, no matter how long you extend them. They are always the same distance apart.
Tools You Need:
- Ruler
- Set square (a triangle-shaped tool with right angles).

This is a rule in geometry:
If corresponding angles are equal, the lines are parallel.

sections.label: Alternate Angles
sections.html:
Alternate Angles

We have two lines I and {tex}{m}{/tex} cut by a third line {tex}{t}{/tex} (this third line is called a transversal).
Look at the diagram with angles {tex}{a , b , c , d , e , f , g , h . ~}{/tex}

Rule:
If the two lines are parallel, then alternate angles are equal.

If {tex}\angle f=120^{\circ}{/tex}, then:
- {tex}\angle {b}=120^{\circ}{/tex} (corresponding angle)
- {tex}\angle {d}=120^{\circ}{/tex} (vertically opposite angle of {tex}\angle {b}{/tex} )

So, {tex}\angle {f}=\angle {d} \rightarrow{/tex} which proves they are alternate angles and equal.
This proves lines {tex}{I}{/tex} and {tex}{m}{/tex} must be parallel if alternate angles are equal.

Concept Key Idea
Parallel Lines by Folding Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel
Set Square Method Drawing {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} angles ensures equal corresponding angles
Alternate Angles If alternate angles are equal, lines must be parallel

sections.label: Parallel Illusions
sections.html:
A parallel illusion happens when your eyes and brain are tricked into thinking lines are not parallel, even though they actually are!

 

In the picture, look carefully at all three designs.

What causes these illusions?
Ans: (a) At first glance, the image looks chaotic, with lines appearing tilted or uneven. However, the vertical lines are actually straight, evenly spaced, and parallel. The diagonal lines, fanning out like spokes, intersect the vertical ones, creating an optical illusion. This illusion tricks our brain into perceiving distortion because the slanted lines and central focal point mislead our sense of alignment.
(b) This pattern seems filled with zigzagging lines and tilted shapes, making it appear uneven. But the white horizontal spaces between the black shapes are truly parallel. The illusion occurs because the bold, slanted black shapes distort our visual perception, making the white lines seem slanted or uneven.
(c) Though the image seems curved and pulled inward, the top and bottom horizontal lines are actually parallel. The radiating diagonal lines from the center create a depth illusion, making the straight lines appear bent. This classic optical illusion deceives our brain into seeing curves where none exist.

Why Do These Illusions Work?
Illusions like these work because of how our brain processes patterns and space:
- Distractions: Other shapes (like zigzags or curves) distract your eyes.
- Angles: Sharp angles make you think lines are changing direction.
- Context: Your brain compares lines to nearby objects.
- Depth Perception: Some images make flat things look 3D (like the tunnel effect).

sections.label: Numbers Tell us Things
sections.html:
Numbers Tell us Things
Numbers are more than tools for counting. They represent relationships and often follow rules that let us infer information about a situation. For example, when people in a line call out numbers that do not match the order they are standing in, those numbers may still be encoding something meaningful about their positions.

What Do These Numbers Actually Mean?

  • One useful interpretation is that each child is saying how many children in front of them are taller than they are. This assumes the height of each child is fixed and known relative to others.
  • So, if the tallest child is red, then in an arrangement each child may call out the number of children taller than them who stand ahead in the line. In other situations the number might mean how many children came before them in line, or how many taller or shorter children are around them. The exact meaning depends on the rule the problem gives, but the key idea is that numbers can encode positional relationships.

sections.label: Picking Parity
sections.html:
Picking Parity:
Parity is the property of a number being either even or odd.
This section explores rules and patterns connected with parity.
Properties of Even and Odd Numbers
- Even numbers: Can be paired with no leftover; examples: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.
- Odd numbers: Always leave one unpaired object when grouped; examples: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 .
- The sum of two odd numbers is even.
- The sum of three odd numbers is odd.

sections.label: Some Explorations in Grids
sections.html:
Some Explorations in Grids

  • In a 3×3 grid with numbers 1-9, sum of all row sums = sum of all column sums = 45.
  • Magic square: each row, column, and diagonal has the same sum.
  • For 3×3 magic square using 1-9, magic sum must be 15.

How to start filling a {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} magic square
One approach is to place three numbers that already add to 15 in one row or column (for example 1, 5, 9 in a row). Then fill the remaining positions using the remaining numbers ( {tex}2,3,4,6,7,8{/tex} ), ensuring each number is used once and that every row, column, and diagonal adds to 15 .

Generalising a {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} magic square (using algebra)
When we generalise in mathematics we look for a pattern or rule that works for all similar cases. For a 3 {tex}\times 3{/tex} magic square made with consecutive numbers (like 1-9), the centre number plays a key role. Call the centre number {tex}{m}{/tex}. For the {tex}1-9{/tex} case, {tex}{m}=5{/tex} and the magic sum {tex}=3 {~m}=15{/tex}.

We can express other entries relative to m (for example {tex}{m} \pm {k}{/tex} ), and there is a standard arrangement that always gives a {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} magic square when using consecutive numbers centred on m .

The First-ever {tex}4 \times 4{/tex} Magic Square

This famous {tex}4 \times 4{/tex} magic square from ancient India is called the Chautīśa Yantra, found in the Parshvanath Jain temple in Khajuraho. "Chautīśā" means 34.

Other patterns that add up to 34
The Chautīśa Yantra is special because many other combinations of four numbers in it also add to 34 .
Examples to check:
- Corners: {tex}7+14+4+9=34{/tex}
- Centre {tex}2 \times 2: 13+8+3+10=34{/tex}
- Same position in different rows: {tex}12+13+3+6=34{/tex}

 

Magic Squares in History and Culture
- Lo Shu Square - the oldest known magic square
The first recorded magic square is called the Lo Shu Square, from ancient China, more than 2000 years ago. 

Where else are magic squares found?
- Magic squares have been studied in India, Japan, China, Central Asia, and Europe.
- Ancient India produced {tex}3 \times 3,4 \times 4,5 \times 5{/tex} and larger magic squares using clever methods.
- They appear in art, architecture and religious diagrams (yantras).

 

What is a Kubera Yantra?
The Kubera Yantra is a sacred diagram associated with Lord Kubera, the deity of wealth. It often contains a magic square inside it.

sections.label: Nature's Favourite Sequence: The Virahāṅka– Fibonacci Numbers!
sections.html:
Nature's Favourite Sequence: The Virahāñka-Fibonacci Numbers
A well-known sequence begins
{tex}1,2,3,5,8,13,21,34, \ldots{/tex}
Each number after the first two is the sum of the two preceding numbers. This list is called the Fibonacci sequence in the West and the Virahānka numbers in India. It appears widely in plants, art and music.

Discovery and origin
Surprisingly, this sequence first appeared in the study of poetry (Sanskrit, Tamil, Telugu and other Indian traditions). Poets composed rhythms using short syllables ( 1 beat) and long syllables ( 2 beats). They asked questions such as: "In how many ways can we fill a line of poetry with {tex}n{/tex} beats using short and long syllables?"
- Virahāñka introduced the numbers in India around {tex}{7 0 0 ~ C E}{/tex}.
- Fibonacci described them in Europe in {tex}{1 2 0 2}{/tex} CE.
- The sequence appears in nature: sunflower spirals, pinecones, pineapples and flowers often show counts equal to Fibonacci numbers (for example 13, 21 or 34 petals).

sections.label: Digits in Disguise
sections.html:
Digits in Disguise (Cryptarithms / Alphametics)
In cryptarithms each letter stands for a digit (0-9) and you must find which digit each letter represents to make the arithmetic correct.

revision.chapter: A Tale of Three Intersecting Lines

sections.label: Equilateral Triangles
sections.html:
• Have you ever looked closely at the world around you? From the slice of cheese you eat to the bridges you cross, straight lines and closed shapes are everywhere. The simplest closed shape made with straight lines is the triangle.

    One of the most fundamental and fascinating shapes is the triangle. It might seem simple, just three lines meeting at three points, but this basic shape holds a surprising amount of depth and is the building block for many complex structures.

  • A triangle is a closed figure formed by three straight line segments joining three non-collinear points. A triangle has three vertices, three sides and three interior angles. In the sections that follow we will study these parts, how to name triangles, how to construct them, when a set of three lengths can form a triangle, and important properties used in solving geometric problems.

A triangle is completely determined by its three vertices and the straight line segments that join them. The triangle with vertices labelled {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} is written as {tex}\Delta {A B C}{/tex}. The three sides are {tex}A B, B C{/tex} and {tex}C A{/tex}. The interior angles at vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C are written as {tex}\angle {A}, \angle {B}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {C}{/tex} respectively.

Vertices, Sides and Angles
- Vertices: the three corner points where sides meet.
- Sides: the three straight line segments joining pairs of vertices.
- Angles: the three interior angles, each formed by two sides meeting at a vertex.

These are the corner points where the sides intersect. Think of them as the 'tips' of the triangle.

Triangles come in many shapes: tall and thin, short and wide, symmetric or completely unequal in their sides and angles.

Naming Triangles
We name a triangle by its three vertex letters. For example, the triangle with vertices {tex}{A}, {B}{/tex} and C is written {tex}\Delta {A B C}{/tex}. The order of vertices may be changed but the same three letters represent the same triangle.

Angles of a Triangle
For {tex}\triangle {ABC}{/tex}, the interior angles are {tex}\angle {A}{/tex} (or {tex}\angle {CAB}{/tex} ), {tex}\angle {B}{/tex} (or {tex}\angle {ABC}{/tex} ) and {tex}\angle {C}{/tex} (or {tex}\angle {BCA}{/tex} ). Often we use the single letter notation {tex}\angle {A}, \angle {B}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {C}{/tex}.

Equilateral Triangles
An equilateral triangle is a triangle with all three sides equal. Equilateral triangles are perfectly symmetric; each interior angle measures {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}.

Because all sides are equal, all angles in an equilateral triangle are also equal (each measuring 60 degrees).

Constructing an Equilateral Triangle
To construct an equilateral triangle with each side 4 cm exactly, use a ruler and a compass. A ruler alone would require trial and error; the compass gives an accurate construction.

sections.label: Constructing a Triangle When its Sides are Given
sections.html:
Constructing a Triangle When Its Three Sides are Given
Given three side lengths, you can construct the triangle (when it exists) using two circles whose radii are the given side lengths from the chosen base endpoints. For example, to construct a triangle with sides 4 {tex}{cm}, 5 {~cm}{/tex} and 6 cm :

 

Steps to construct the triangle with sides {tex}{4 ~ c m}, 5 {c m}{/tex} and {tex}{6 c m}{/tex}
1. Draw the base: Choose one side to be the base; {tex}\operatorname{draw} {AB}=4 {~cm}{/tex} and label its endpoints A and B.

2. Draw an arc from A: With the compass set to 5 cm , place the needle at A and draw an arc showing all points 5 cm from A .

3. Draw an arc from B : With the compass set to 6 cm , place the needle at B and draw an arc showing all points 6 cm from B .

4. Locate their intersection: The intersection point of the two arcs is {tex}C{/tex}. That point is 5 cm from {tex}A{/tex} and 6 cm from B.


5. Join the points: Draw AC and BC to complete triangle {tex}\triangle {ABC}{/tex} with sides {tex}4 {~cm}, 5 {~cm}{/tex} and 6 cm .

When is a Triangle Possible? Triangle Inequality Theorem
Not every three positive lengths can be the sides of a triangle. The Triangle Inequality Theorem states:
For any triangle, the sum of the lengths of any two sides must be greater than the length of the remaining side.

Examples to test
- Try to make a triangle with side lengths {tex}3 {~cm}, 4 {~cm}{/tex} and 8 cm .
- Try to make a triangle with side lengths {tex}2 {~cm}, 3 {~cm}{/tex} and 6 cm .
- Try to make a triangle with side lengths {tex}10 {~cm}, 15 {~cm}{/tex} and 30 cm .

In case of these examples the sum of two shorter sides is equal to or less than the longest side, so a triangle cannot be formed.

Efficient Way to Check Triangle Possibility:
Given three lengths a,b,c. A triangle is only possible
if:

{tex} a+b>c, b+c>a, c+a>b {/tex}

Circle construction viewpoint:
Another way to see the triangle inequality is by the circle method used in constructions. Take the longest side AB as fixed. Draw a circle with centre A and radius equal to one of the other given lengths, and {tex}a{/tex} circle with centre B and radius equal to the remaining length. The two circles will intersect in two points only when the sum of the two radii is greater than AB . The three possibilities are:
- Case 1 - circles touch each other externally: radius1 + radius2 = AB. The "triangle" collapses to a straight line (no area), so a triangle does not form.
- Case 2 - circles are separate (do not meet): radius1 + radius2 < AB. There is no common point and therefore no possible third vertex C; a triangle cannot be formed.
- Case 3 - circles intersect at two points: radius1+radius2 > AB. The intersection points give possible positions for the third vertex C , and a triangle can be formed.

sections.label: Construction of Triangles When Some Sides and Angles are Given
sections.html:
Constructing Triangles When Some Sides and Angles are Given
-Two sides and the included angle (SAS)
When two sides of a triangle and the angle between them are given, the triangle can often be constructed using ruler, compass and protractor. This is called the "two sides and the included angle" (SAS) condition.

-Two angles and the included side (ASA)
If two angles and the side between them are given, the triangle can usually be constructed because the third angle is fixed by the angle sum property. This case is called ASA (angle-side-angle).

Angle Sum Property
The sum of the interior angles of any triangle is {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}.

Exterior Angle Theorem
An exterior angle of a triangle is formed by extending one of its sides. The exterior angle is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite (non-adjacent) angles.

sections.label: Constructions Related to Altitudes of Triangles
sections.html:
Constructions Related to Altitudes (Heights) of Triangles
An altitude of a triangle is a perpendicular drawn from a vertex to the opposite side (or its extension). The length of this perpendicular is called the height of the triangle from that vertex.

sections.label: Types of Triangles
sections.html:
Types of Triangles
Based on sides
1. Equilateral triangle:
- All three sides equal.
- All three angles equal, each {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}.
2. Isosceles triangle:
- Two sides equal.
- The angles opposite the equal sides are equal.
3. Scalene triangle:
- All three sides of different lengths.
- All three angles are different.

4. Right-angled triangle:
- One angle is exactly {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}.
- The side opposite the right angle is the hypotenuse; the other two sides are legs.

sections.label: Multiplication of Fractions
sections.html:
Multiplication of Fractions:
Multiplication is often thought of as "repeated addition." This stays true for fractions!
Multiplying a fraction by a whole number

To multiply a fraction by a whole number, you multiply the numerator (top) by the whole number and keep the denominator (bottom) the same.

  • Rule: {tex}n \times \frac{a}{b} = \frac{n \times a}{b}{/tex}

  • Example: If a tortoise walks {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} km in 1 hour, in 3 hours it walks {tex}3 \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} km.

Multiplying a Fraction by another Fraction

This is like finding a "part of a part." For example, half of a quarter ({tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{4}{/tex}).

  • The Golden Rule: Multiply the top numbers together and the bottom numbers together.

  • Formula (Brahmagupta’s Formula): {tex}\frac{a}{b} \times \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a \times c}{b \times d}{/tex}

  • Example: {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5} = \frac{2 \times 4}{3 \times 5} = \frac{8}{15}{/tex}

  • Simplifying (Cancelling): Before multiplying big numbers, check if any top number and bottom number share a common factor. Divide them out first to make the math easier!

  • Is the product always bigger? No!

    • If you multiply by a number between 0 and 1, the result gets smaller.

    • If you multiply by a number greater than 1, the result gets bigger.

sections.label: Division of Fractions
sections.html:
Divisions of Fractions:
Division is simply the "reverse" of multiplication. To divide fractions, we use a special tool called the Reciprocal.To find the reciprocal of a fraction, just "flip" it upside down.

What is Reciprocal?

  • The reciprocal of {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}.

  • The reciprocal of {tex}5{/tex} (which is {tex}\frac{5}{1}{/tex}) is {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}.

  • A fraction multiplied by its reciprocal always equals 1.

How to Divide

To divide by a fraction, you multiply by its reciprocal.

  • Formula: {tex}\frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a}{b} \times \frac{d}{c}{/tex}

  • Example: {tex}\frac{2}{3} \div \frac{3}{5} \rightarrow \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{3} = \frac{10}{9}{/tex}

Fun Fact about Division:

When you divide a number by a proper fraction (like {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}), the answer actually becomes larger than the starting number!

  • Example: {tex}6 \div \frac{1}{4} = 24{/tex}. (Think: "How many quarters are in 6 wholes?")

sections.label: Some Problems Involving Fractions
sections.html:
Fractions show up in history and daily life all the time!

  • Area of Rectangles: If a rectangle has sides that are fractions, its area is still Length × Breadth.

    • Example: A rectangle with sides {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m and {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} m has an area of {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{8}{/tex} sq. m.

  • Sharing Quantities: If 5 people share {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} litre of milk, each person gets: {tex}\frac{1}{4} \div 5 = \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{5} = \frac{1}{20}{/tex} litre.

  • Did you know that these rules for fractions were written down by an Indian mathematician named Brahmagupta in 628 CE? Later, Bhāskara II wrote a famous book called Līlāvatī that used fun poems and riddles to teach these same fraction rules!

sections.label: Geometric Twins
sections.html:
Two figures are congruent if they are exact copies of each other. If you were to cut one out and place it over the other, they would fit perfectly. This is called superimposition.

  • Key Idea: Congruent figures have the same measurements. For example, to recreate a specific angle symbol, you need both the lengths of its arms and the measure of the angle between them.

  • Rotation and Flipping: Two shapes are still congruent even if one is turned upside down, rotated, or flipped like a mirror image. As long as the dimensions are the same, they are "twins."

  • Simple Shapes: * Two circles are congruent if they have the same radius.

    • Two rectangles are congruent if they have the same length and breadth.

sections.label: Congruence of Triangles
sections.html:
Congruence of Triangles:
Triangles are special because we don't need to measure all six parts (3 sides and 3 angles) to prove they are congruent. There are five specific conditions that guarantee two triangles are identical:

  1. SSS (Side-Side-Side): All three sides of one triangle are equal to the three sides of another.

  2. SAS (Side-Angle-Side): Two sides and the included angle (the angle between them) are equal.

    Note: SSA (Side-Side-Angle) does not guarantee congruence!

  3. ASA (Angle-Side-Angle): Two angles and the included side (the side between them) are equal.

  4. AAS (Angle-Angle-Side): Two angles and a side not between them are equal.

  5. RHS (Right-angle-Hypotenuse-Side): In right-angled triangles, if the hypotenuse and one other side are equal, they are congruent.

Writing Congruence: We use the symbol {tex}\cong{/tex}. The order of letters matters! If {tex}\Delta ABC \cong \Delta XYZ{/tex}, it means Vertex {tex}A{/tex} matches {tex}X{/tex}, {tex}B{/tex} matches {tex}Y{/tex}, and {tex}C{/tex} matches {tex}Z{/tex}. These are called corresponding parts.

sections.label: Angles of Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles
sections.html:
Angles of Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles:
We can use congruence to prove rules about special triangles:

  • Isosceles Triangles: These have at least two equal sides.

    • The Rule: Angles opposite to equal sides are also equal. If {tex}AB = AC{/tex} in {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex}, then {tex}\angle B = \angle C{/tex}.

  • Equilateral Triangles: All three sides are equal.

    • The Rule: Since all sides are equal, all angles must be equal.

    • The Measurement: Because the sum of angles in a triangle is {tex}180^\circ{/tex}, each angle in an equilateral triangle is exactly {tex}60^\circ{/tex} ({tex}180^\circ \div 3 = 60^\circ{/tex}).

sections.label: A Quick Recap of Integers
sections.html:
Sum and Difference Puzzle:
A common way to practice integers is finding two numbers based on their sum (adding them) and difference (subtracting them).

  • Example: Sum = 25, Difference = 11. The numbers are 18 and 7.

  • Example: Sum = 25, Difference = –11. The numbers are 7 and 18.

  • Rule: Swapping the order of numbers in a subtraction changes the sign of the difference!

The Number Line Model

  • Rightward movement is represented by positive integers.

  • Leftward movement is represented by negative integers.

  • Final Position formula: {tex}P = a + b{/tex}. If you move 5 units right (+5) and then 7 units left (–7), your position is {tex}5 + (-7) = -2{/tex}.

Subtraction and Aditive Inverse

  • Subtracting a number is the same as adding its additive inverse (its opposite).

  • Examples:

    • {tex}7 - 18 = 7 + (-18) = -11{/tex}

    • {tex}4 - (-12) = 4 + 12 = 16{/tex}

  • The additive inverse of {tex}a{/tex} is {tex}-a{/tex}. The additive inverse of {tex}-18{/tex} is {tex}-(-18) = 18{/tex}.

To understand multiplication, we use the Token Model (Green tokens = +1, Red tokens = –1).

A. Interpreting Multiplication

  1. Positive {tex}\times{/tex} Positive: {tex}4 \times 2{/tex} means putting 2 positive tokens into a bag 4 times. Result: +8.

  2. Positive {tex}\times{/tex} Negative: {tex}4 \times (-2){/tex} means putting 2 negative tokens into a bag 4 times. Result: –8.

  3. Negative {tex}\times{/tex} Positive: {tex}(-4) \times 2{/tex} means removing 2 positive tokens from a bag 4 times. To do this from an empty bag, you use zero pairs. Result: –8.

  4. Negative {tex}\times{/tex} Negative: {tex}(-4) \times (-2){/tex} means removing 2 negative tokens from a bag 4 times. Result: +8.

sections.label: Multiplication of Integers
sections.html:
The Rules of Signs (Brahmagupta's Rules)

The 7th-century mathematician Brahmagupta defined these rules clearly:

  • Same Signs {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Positive Product:  {tex}(+) \times (+) = (+){/tex}

    • {tex}(-) \times (-) = (+){/tex}

  • Different Signs {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Negative Product:

    • {tex}(+) \times (-) = (-){/tex}

    • {tex}(-) \times (+) = (-){/tex}

Properties of Multiplication

  • Commutative Property: The order doesn't matter. {tex}a \times b = b \times a{/tex}.

  • Multiplicative Identity: Any integer multiplied by 1 stays the same. {tex}a \times 1 = a{/tex}.

  • Multiplying by –1: Any integer multiplied by –1 becomes its additive inverse. {tex}-1 \times a = -a{/tex}.

  • Associative Property: When multiplying three numbers, you can group them any way. {tex}(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c){/tex}.

  • Distributive Property: Multiplication "distributes" over addition. {tex}a \times (b + c) = (a \times b) + (a \times c){/tex}.

Division of Integers
Division is the inverse of multiplication. The same sign rules apply!

  • Positive {tex}\div{/tex} Negative or Negative {tex}\div{/tex} Positive = Negative

    • Example: {tex}36 \div (-18) = -2{/tex}

  • Negative {tex}\div{/tex} Negative = Positive

    • Example: {tex}(-46) \div (-23) = 2{/tex}

sections.label: The Greatest of All
sections.html:
The Highest Common Factor (HCF), also known as the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD), is the largest number that divides two or more numbers exactly.

  • Real-Life Application: Finding the largest possible tile size to cover a floor without gaps, or the largest bag weight to pack different quantities of goods equally.

  • Prime Factorisation Method: 1. Write the prime factorisation of each number.

    2. Identify the common prime factors.

    3. For each common prime, take the minimum number of occurrences found in any of the numbers.

    4. Multiply these together to get the HCF.

  • Example: HCF of 30 and 72

    • {tex}30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5{/tex}

    • {tex}72 = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3{/tex}

    • Common primes: One {tex}2{/tex} and one {tex}3{/tex}.

    • {tex}HCF = 2 \times 3 = 6{/tex}.

sections.label: Least, but not Last!
sections.html:
The Lowest Common Multiple (LCM) is the smallest number that is a multiple of two or more numbers.

  • Real-Life Application: Finding when two events that happen at different intervals will occur at the same time again (e.g., two people with different step lengths finishing at the same spot).

  • Prime Factorisation Method:

    1. Write the prime factorisation of each number.

    2. List all prime factors that appear in any of the numbers.

    3. For each prime, take the maximum number of occurrences found in any one factorisation.

    4. Multiply them all together to get the LCM.

  • Example: LCM of 14 and 35

    • {tex}14 = 2 \times 7{/tex}

    • {tex}35 = 5 \times 7{/tex}

    • Primes involved: {tex}2, 5, 7{/tex}.

    • {tex}LCM = 2 \times 5 \times 7 = 70{/tex}.

sections.label: Patterns, Properties, and a Pretty Procedure!
sections.html:
• The Product Formula: For any two numbers:

    {tex}\text{HCF} \times \text{LCM} = \text{Product of the two numbers}{/tex}

  • Co-prime Numbers: If two numbers have no common factors other than 1 (e.g., 8 and 9), their HCF is 1 and their LCM is their product ({tex}8 \times 9 = 72{/tex}).

  • Consecutive Numbers: The HCF of any two consecutive numbers (like 14 and 15) is always 1.

Efficient Common Division Method
You can find both HCF and LCM simultaneously by dividing the numbers by common factors:

  1. Divide both numbers by a common factor (starting with primes or any obvious factor).

  2. The HCF is the product of the factors you divided by (the "left side" of the ladder).

  3. The LCM is the product of those factors AND the remaining quotients at the bottom.

sections.label: A Quick Recap of Decimals
sections.html:
Decimals represent fractions with denominators like 10, 100, and 1000.

  • Place Value: In {tex}27.53{/tex}, 5 is in the tenths place ({tex}\frac{5}{10}{/tex}) and 3 is in the hundredths place ({tex}\frac{3}{100}{/tex}).

  • Dividing by 10, 100, 1000: Move the decimal point to the left by the number of zeros in the divisor.

    • Example: {tex}123 \div 100 = 1.23{/tex}

  • In this chapter, we learnt procedures to perform decimal multiplication and division.
  • For decimal multiplication, we first multiply the multiplier and multiplicand as counting numbers. The number of decimal digits in the product is the total number of decimal digits in the multiplier and multiplicand.
  • Division of decimals uses the same procedure, i.e., division using place value (long division), as with counting numbers. The regrouping continues after the Ones place to Tenths, Hundredths, Thousandths, and so on. When the Ones are regrouped to Tenths, a decimal point is placed in the quotient.
  • There are decimal divisions where the quotient never ends. After each regrouping and dividing there is always a remainder!

sections.label: Decimal Multiplication
sections.html:
Decimal Multiplication:
Multiplication of decimals is similar to multiplying whole numbers, with a final step to place the decimal point.

  1. Multiply the numbers as if the decimal points are not there.

  2. Count the total number of decimal places in both the numbers being multiplied.

  3. Place the decimal point in the product so that it has the same total number of decimal places.

  • Example: {tex}5.96 \times 24.8{/tex}

    • {tex}596 \times 248 = 147808{/tex}

    • Total decimal places: {tex}2{/tex} (in {tex}5.96{/tex}) {tex}+ 1{/tex} (in {tex}24.8{/tex}) {tex}= 3{/tex}.

    • Product: {tex}147.808{/tex}

  • If both numbers are {tex}> 1{/tex}, the product is greater than both.

  • If both numbers are between 0 and 1, the product is smaller than both (e.g., {tex}0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25{/tex}).

  • If one is {tex}> 1{/tex} and the other is between 0 and 1, the product is in between them.

sections.label: Decimal Division
sections.html:
Dividing a Decimal by a Whole Number:
Use "Long Division" (place value division). When you reach the decimal point in the dividend, place a decimal point in the quotient.

  • Example: {tex}9.5 \div 4 = 2.375{/tex}

Dividing by 10, 100, 1000

Move the decimal point to the left.

  • {tex}18.7 \div 100 = 0.187{/tex}

Dividing by a Decimal Divisor

Convert the divisor into a whole number by multiplying both the dividend and the divisor by 10, 100, or 1000 (shifting the decimal point to the right).

  • Example: {tex}4.68 \div 1.3{/tex}

    • Multiply both by 10: {tex}46.8 \div 13 = 3.6{/tex}

Non-Terminating Decimals

Some divisions never end and repeat a pattern.

  • {tex}10 \div 3 = 3.333...{/tex}

  • {tex}1 \div 7 = 0.142857142857...{/tex} (The block {tex}142857{/tex} repeats).

sections.label: Look Before You Leap!
sections.html:
The Earth takes approximately 365.2422 days to orbit the Sun. Our calendar uses 365 days, creating a discrepancy that we fix with Leap Years.

The Leap Year Rules:

  1. Every 4 years: Add 1 day (Year must be divisible by 4).

  2. The Century Exception: Years divisible by 100 are NOT leap years...

  3. The 400-Year Rule: ...unless they are also divisible by 400 (e.g., 2000 was a leap year, but 1900 was not).

sections.label: Of Questions and Statements
sections.html:
:A statistical question is the starting point of any data investigation. Its defining characteristic is variability.

  • Anticipating Variety: When you ask a statistical question, you do not expect a single, uniform answer. For example, if you ask, "How much time do students in my class spend on homework?" you know the answer will vary from 10 minutes to 2 hours.

  • The Need for Collection: Because there are many different answers, you cannot simply look up one fact. You must collect a dataset from a group (a population or a sample) to see the full picture.

  • Purpose: These questions aim to describe a "typical" value, the "spread" of values, or the "shape" of the data distribution.

  • Examples: * "What are the favorite colors of students in the primary wing?" (Variability exists because different students like different colors).

    • "What was the daily high temperature in Mumbai last July?" (Variability exists because the temperature changes day to day).

Statistical Statements:
Once data has been collected and analyzed, the findings are communicated through statistical statements. These are claims that summarize complex information into a digestible format.

  • Numerical Summaries: These often use measures like the Mean (Average), Median, or Range. Instead of listing 100 individual heights, you might say, "The average height is 150 cm."

  • Proportions and Percentages: Statements often describe how common something is. For example, "Three out of four students prefer cricket over football."

  • Predictions and Probabilities: Statistics allow us to look at past data to guess future events. A statement like, "There is a 70% chance of rain today," is based on historical weather patterns.

  • Contextual Claims: These statements give meaning to numbers. For example, saying "The population of our village has reduced by 100 in the last decade" summarizes ten years of census data into one trend.

Statistical vs. Non- Statistical:
The easiest way to tell if a question or statement is "statistical" is to look for Data Variability.

  • Non-Statistical Questions (Deterministic): These have a single, specific, and "fixed" answer. There is no "data" to collect because there is no variation.

    • Example: "How many sides does a triangle have?" The answer is always 3. It doesn't matter who you ask or when you ask it; the answer never varies.

    • Example: "Who is the current Prime Minister of India?" There is only one correct answer at any given moment.

  • The "Typical" Test: Statistical questions often imply the word "typically" or "generally." * Non-statistical: "How old is that specific dog?" (One answer).

    • Statistical: "How old are the dogs that live on this street?" (Many answers; requires an average or range to describe "typically").

  • Dot plots help us get a quick glimpse of the variability of the dataminimum, maximum, range, and how data is clustered or spread out.
  • The Arithmetic Mean {tex}=\frac{\text { Sum of all the values in the data }}{\text { Number of values in the data }}{/tex}.
  • The Median is the number in the middle of any sorted data. If there are an even number of values, then the median is the average of the two middle numbers.
  • We can describe and compare data in several ways including by referring to the minimum, maximum, total, range, arithmetic mean, and median.
  • We learnt how to read and make clustered bar graphs. These graphs can be used to compare and visualise values across categories and across time.
  • Examining data can lead to new questions and directions to probe further.

Outliers:

  • Definition: Values that significantly deviate from the rest of the data (much higher or much lower).

  • Impact: Outliers pull the Mean toward them (e.g., a very high outlier makes the mean larger than most data points).

    • The Median is generally not affected by outliers.

sections.label: Representative Values
sections.html:
When comparing groups of data, a single number can often represent the whole group.

Arithmetic Mean:

  • Definition: The sum of all values divided by the total number of values.

  • Concept: Ancient Indian mathematicians called this samamiti (mean measure) or samīkaraṇa (equalizing). It represents a "fair share" if everything were distributed equally.

  • Formula: {tex}\text{Mean} = \frac{\text{Sum of all values}}{\text{Number of values}}{/tex}

Median:

  • Definition: The middle value when data is sorted in ascending or descending order.

  • Calculation:

    • If the number of values is odd, the median is the exact middle number.

    • If the number of values is even, the median is the average of the two middle numbers.

  • Benefit: The median is a better representative than the mean when the data contains outliers.

sections.label: Visualising Data
sections.html:
Visualizing Data:
Visualizations help identify patterns and variability that tables of numbers might hide.

Dot Plots

  • Shows data points as dots on a number line.

  • Utility: Helps visualize "clusters" (where most data sits) and "spread" (how far apart the values are).

  • Variability: You can see the Range (difference between maximum and minimum values) easily.

Clustered Column Graphs (Double Bar Graphs)

  • Draws bars side-by-side to compare two or more groups across categories (e.g., comparing onion prices in two different cities across twelve months).

  • Scale: The choice of scale (e.g., 1 unit = 20 rockets) is crucial for accurate reading.

  • 2-Step Analysis Process:

    1. Identify: Note the labels, scale, categories, and general patterns.

    2. Infer: Interpret what those patterns mean (e.g., "USA, China, and Russia are the leading rocket launchers").

sections.label: Data Detective
sections.html:
Data Detective:
Statistics is not just about calculating numbers; it's about asking "Why?"

  • Seasonality: Data patterns (like onion prices or daylight hours) often correlate with the time of year.

  • Zero vs. No Value: In statistics, a score of 0 is a piece of data (it impacts the mean). A missing value (where someone didn't participate) is ignored and reduces the total count used for the average.

  • Historical Context: Land measurements like the "rod" (16 feet) were originally created by taking the Mean length of 16 different men's feet.

sections.label: Geometric Constructions
sections.html:
Geometric construction is the process of drawing shapes using only two tools: an unmarked ruler (straightedge) and a compass.

A. Perpendicular Bisector

  • Definition: A line that divides a line segment into two equal parts (bisection) and meets it at a 90° angle (perpendicular).

  • Key Property: Any point that is at an equal distance from the endpoints {tex}X{/tex} and {tex}Y{/tex} of a segment must lie on its perpendicular bisector.

  • Construction Method:

    1. Set the compass to a radius greater than half the length of {tex}XY{/tex}.

    2. Draw arcs from {tex}X{/tex} and {tex}Y{/tex} that intersect above the line (Point {tex}A{/tex}) and below the line (Point {tex}B{/tex}).

    3. Connect {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex}. Line {tex}AB{/tex} is the perpendicular bisector.

  • Historical Context: The Śulba-Sūtras (ancient Indian geometric texts) describe using a rope as a compass and straightedge to construct fire altars with these same principles.

Angle Bisection:

  • Definition: Dividing an angle into two smaller, equal angles.

  • Construction Method:

    1. From the vertex {tex}O{/tex}, draw an arc intersecting both arms of the angle at points {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} (so {tex}OA = OB{/tex}).

    2. From {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex}, draw arcs of equal radius that intersect at point {tex}C{/tex}.

    3. Line {tex}OC{/tex} is the angle bisector.

  • Applications: Constructing a 45° angle by bisecting a 90° angle, or a 30° angle by bisecting a 60° angle.

Copying Angles and Parallel Lines

  • Copying an Angle: To copy {tex}\angle A{/tex}, you create a congruent triangle using arcs to "measure" the distance between the angle's arms and transferring that distance to a new vertex.Parallel Lines: You can construct a line parallel to a given line by creating equal corresponding angles using the angle-copying method.
  • Parallel Lines: You can construct a line parallel to a given line by creating equal corresponding angles using the angle-copying method.

Regular Hexagons and 60° Angles

  • 60° Angle: Constructed by forming an equilateral triangle. Since all sides of an equilateral triangle are equal, you use the same compass radius for all arcs.
  • Regular Hexagon: Can be viewed as six congruent equilateral triangles meeting at a center point. Because {tex}6 \times 60^\circ = 360^\circ{/tex}, they fit perfectly around a point without gaps or overlaps.
  • A division of a line segment, or any geometrical quantity, into two identical parts is called bisection.
  • Any point that is of equal distance from the two endpoints of a given line segment lies on its perpendicular bisector. This property can be used to construct the perpendicular bisector using a ruler and compass.
  • The method of constructing the perpendicular bisector can be modified to draw a {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex} angle at any point on a line using only a ruler and compass.
  • An angle can be bisected and copied using the congruence properties of triangles.
  • A {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex} angle can be constructed using a ruler and compass by constructing an equilateral triangle.
  • Covering a region using a set of shapes, without gaps or overlaps, is called tiling.

sections.label: Tiling
sections.html:
Tiling is the act of covering a flat surface (plane) with geometric shapes so that there are no gaps and no overlaps.

Grid Tiling:

  • Rule: A {tex}2 \times 1{/tex} tile (like a domino) can tile an {tex}m \times n{/tex} grid only if the total number of unit squares ({tex}m \times n{/tex}) is even.

  • The Coloring Trick: By coloring a grid like a chessboard (black and white), we can solve complex tiling puzzles.

    • Each {tex}2 \times 1{/tex} tile must cover one black square and one white square.

    • If a region has an unequal number of black and white squares (e.g., 8 white and 6 black), it is impossible to tile.

Tiling the Entire Plane

  • Regular Polygons: Only three regular polygons can tile the plane by themselves:

    1. Squares ({tex}90^\circ \times 4 = 360^\circ{/tex})

    2. Equilateral Triangles ({tex}60^\circ \times 6 = 360^\circ{/tex})

    3. Regular Hexagons ({tex}120^\circ \times 3 = 360^\circ{/tex})

  • In Nature: Honeybees use hexagonal tiling for their hives because it is the most efficient way to store honey using the least amount of wax without wasting space.

sections.label: Find the Unknowns
sections.html:
• An algebraic equation is a mathematical statement that indicates the equality of two algebraic expressions.
  • When the same operation is performed on both sides of an equation, equality is maintained.
  • Finding a solution to an equation means finding the values of the unknowns in the expressions such that the LHS is equal to the RHS.
  • Equations can often be solved by performing the same operation on both sides so that the value of the unknown becomes evident.

An equation is like a balanced weighing scale. For the scale to remain level, the total weight on the Left-Hand Side (LHS) must equal the Right-Hand Side (RHS).

The golen rule of Equations:

To maintain balance, whatever operation you perform on one side, you must perform on the other:

  • Addition: If {tex}x = y{/tex}, then {tex}x + a = y + a{/tex}.

  • Subtraction: If {tex}x = y{/tex}, then {tex}x - a = y - a{/tex}.

  • Multiplication/Division: If {tex}x = y{/tex}, then {tex}ax = ay{/tex} (for {tex}a \neq 0{/tex}).

sections.label: Solving Equations Systematically
sections.html:
Solving an equation means finding the value of the "letter-number" (variable) that makes the statement true.

A. Trial and Error Method

Substituting different numbers until the LHS equals the RHS.

  • Example: For {tex}2n + 1 = 99{/tex}, you might try {tex}n=10, 40, 50{/tex} until you find {tex}n=49{/tex}.

  • Limitation: It is slow and inefficient for complex problems.

Systematic Method (Inverse Operations):
We "undo" operations to isolate the unknown variable.

  1. Undo Addition with Subtraction: {tex}x + 7 = 21 \implies x = 21 - 7{/tex}

  2. Undo Subtraction with Addition: {tex}x - 4 = 10 \implies x = 10 + 4{/tex}

  3. Undo Multiplication with Division: {tex}5x = 20 \implies x = 20 \div 5{/tex}

  4. Undo Division with Multiplication: {tex}\frac{u}{15} = 6 \implies u = 6 \times 15{/tex}

sections.label: Mind the Mistake, Mend the Mistake
sections.html:
Mind the Mistake, Mend the Mistake

Wrong Step

 The Mistake Correction
{tex}4x + 6 = 10 \implies 4x = 10 + 6{/tex} Added instead of subtracting 6. {tex}4x = 10 - 6{/tex}
{tex}3(x + 1) = 12 \implies 3x + 1 = 12{/tex} Forgot to multiply the 1 by 3. {tex}3x + 3 = 12{/tex}
{tex}x + 9 = 66 \implies x = 11{/tex} Divided only the 66, not the 9. Subtract 9 first, then divide.

sections.label: A Pinch of History
sections.html:
Ancient Indian mathematicians were pioneers in Algebra (Bījagāṇita). Brahmagupta (628 CE) provided a direct formula for equations in the form:

{tex}Ax + B = Cx + D{/tex}

The solution is:

{tex}x = \frac{D - B}{A - C}{/tex}

Example: Solve {tex}5x + 4 = 3x + 8{/tex}

  • {tex}A=5, B=4, C=3, D=8{/tex}

  • {tex}x = \frac{8 - 4}{5 - 3} = \frac{4}{2} = 2{/tex}.

sections.label: In-Text
sections.html:
Q 1

What if we ate 2 varieties of rice every day? Would we then be able to eat 1 lakh varieties of rice in 100 years?

Solution

Days in a year {tex}=365{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten per day {tex}=2{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten in a year {tex}=2 \times 365=730{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten in 100 years {tex}=730 \times 100=73,000{/tex} varieties.
No, we can eat only 73,000 varieties in 100 years, not 1 lakh.

Q 2

What if a person ate 3 varieties of rice every day? Will they be able to taste all the lakh varieties in a 100 year lifetime? Find out.

Solution

Days in a year {tex}=365{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten per day {tex}=3{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten in a year {tex}=3 \times 365=1095{/tex}
Rice varieties eaten in 100 years {tex}=1095 \times 100=1,09,500{/tex} varieties.
Yes, they can eat all 1 lakh varieties in 100 years.

Q 3

Estu said, "We know how many days there are in a year - 365, if we ignore leap years. If we live for {tex}y{/tex} years, the number of days in our lifetime will be {tex}365 \times y{/tex}."

Solution

Assume we live for 95 years, i.e. {tex}{y}=95{/tex}.
Number of days in our lifetime {tex}=365 \times 95=34,675{/tex}
Difference between {tex}1,00,000{/tex} and {tex}34,675=1,00,000-34,675=65,325{/tex}.

Q 4

Look at the picture on the right. Somu is 1 metre tall. If each floor is about four times his height, what is the approximate height of the building?

Solution

Somu's height = 1 metre
Height of each floor {tex}=4 \times 1=4{/tex} metres
Number of floors in the building {tex}=10{/tex}
Height of the building {tex}=10 \times 4=40{/tex} metres.

Q 5

Which is taller - The Statue of Unity or this building? How much taller? ________ m.

Solution

Height of The Statue of Unity = 180 m
Height of Somu's building {tex}=40 {~m}{/tex}
Since, {tex}180-40=140 {~m}{/tex}
The Statue of Unity is 140 m taller than the building.

Q 6

How much taller is the Kunchikal waterfall than Somu's building? ________ m.

Solution

Height of the Kunchikal waterfall {tex}=450 {~m}{/tex}
Height of Somu's building {tex}=40 {~m}{/tex}
Since {tex}450-40=410 {~m}{/tex}
The Kunchikal waterfall is 410 m taller than Somu's building.

Q 7

How many floors should Somu’s building have to be as high as the waterfall?

Solution

Height of each floor in the building {tex}=4 {~m}{/tex}
Height of the waterfall {tex}=450 {~m}{/tex}
Number of floors required {tex}=450 \div 4=112.5{/tex}
So, Somu's building should have at least 113 floors to be as high as the waterfall.

Q 8

How do you view a lakh - is a lakh big or small?

Solution

A lakh can seem big or small depending on the context and what it's being compared to. For instance, a crowd of 1 lakh people at an event or protest is massive, but in terms of city population, 1 lakh might be considered a small town.

Q 9

Write each of the numbers given below in words:

  1. {tex} 3,00,600 {/tex}
  2. {tex} 5,04,085 {/tex}
  3. {tex} 27,30,000 {/tex}
  4. {tex} 70,53,138 {/tex}
Solution

{tex}3,00,600{/tex} - Three lakh six hundred.
5,04,085 - Five lakh four thousand eighty five.
27,30,000 - Twenty seven lakh thirty thousand.
{tex}70,53,138{/tex} - Seventy lakh fifty three thousand one hundred and thirty eight.

Q 10

Write the corresponding number in the Indian place value system for each of the following:

  1. One lakh twenty three thousand four hundred and fifty six
  2. Four lakh seven thousand seven hundred and four
  3. Fifty lakhs five thousand and fifty
  4. Ten lakhs two hundred and thirty five
Solution
  1. One lakh twenty three thousand four hundred and fifty six {tex}-1,23,456{/tex}.
  2. Four lakh seven thousand seven hundred and four - 4,07,704.
  3. Fifty lakhs five thousand and fifty - 50,05,050.
  4. Ten lakhs two hundred and thirty five - {tex}10,02,035{/tex}.
Q 11

The Thoughtful Thousands only has a +1000 button. How many times should it be pressed to show:

  1. Three thousand? 3 times
  2. 10,000? ________
  3. Fifty three thousand? ________
  4. 90,000? ________
  5. One Lakh? ________
  6. ________? 153 times
  7. How many thousands are required to make one lakh?
Solution
  1. {tex} 10 \text { times }(\because 10,000 \div 1000=10) {/tex}
  2. {tex} 53 \text { times }(\because 53,000 \div 1000=53) {/tex}
  3. {tex} 90 \text { times }(\because 90,000 \div 1000=90) {/tex}
  4. {tex} 100 \text { times }(\because 1,00,000 \div 1000=100) {/tex}
  5. {tex} 1,53,000(\because 153 \times 1000=1,53,000) {/tex}
  6. {tex} 1,00,000 \div 1000=100 . {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} 100 thousands make one lakh.
Q 12

The Tedious Tens only has a +10 button. How many times should it be pressed to show:

  1. Five hundred?
  2. 780? ________
  3. 1000? ________
  4. 3700? ________
  5. 10,000? ________
  6. One lakh? ________
  7. ________? 435 times
Solution
  1. {tex} 50 \text { times }(\because 500 \div 10=50) {/tex}
  2. {tex} 78 \text { times }(\because 780 \div 10=78) {/tex}
  3. {tex} 100 \text { times }(\because 1000 \div 10=100) {/tex}
  4. {tex} 370 \text { times }(\because 3700 \div 10=370) {/tex}
  5. {tex} 1000 \text { times }(\because 10,000 \div 10=1,000) {/tex}
  6. {tex} 10000 \text { times }(\because 1,00,00 \div 10=10,000) {/tex}
  7. {tex} 4350(\because 435 \times 10=4350) {/tex}
Q 13

The Handy Hundreds only has a +100 button. How many times should it be pressed to show:

  1. Four hundred? ________ times
  2. 3,700? ________
  3. 10,000? ________
  4. Fifty three thousand? ________
  5. 90,000? ________
  6. 97,600? ________
  7. 1,00,000? ________
  8. ________? 582 times
  9. How many hundreds are required to make ten thousand?
  10. How many hundreds are required to make one lakh?
  11. Handy Hundreds says, “There are some numbers which Tedious Tens and Thoughtful Thousands can’t show but I can.” Is this statement true? Think and explore.
Solution
  1. {tex} 4 \text { times }(\because 400 \div 100=4) {/tex}
  2. {tex} 37 \text { times }(\because 3700 \div 100=37) {/tex}
  3. {tex} 100 \text { times }(\because 10,000 \div 100=100) {/tex}
  4. {tex} 530 \text { times }(\because 53,000 \div 100=530) {/tex}
  5. {tex} 900 \text { times }(\because 90,000 \div 100=900) {/tex}
  6. {tex} 976 \text { times }(\because 97,600 \div 100=976) {/tex} + {tex} 1000 \text { times }(\because 1,00,000 \div 100=1,000) {/tex}
  7. {tex} 58,200(\because 582 \times 100=58,200) {/tex}
  8. {tex} \frac{10,000}{100}=100 \text { hundreds } {/tex}
    100 hundreds make ten thousand.
  9. {tex} \frac{1,00,000}{100}=1000 \text { hundreds } {/tex}
  10. 100 hundreds make one lakh.
  11. Handy Hundreds' statement is true compared to Thoughtful Thousands because Thoughtful Thousands can’t show multiples of 100 or 10. However, Handy Hundreds' statement is false compared to Tedious Tens because Tedious Tens can reach any multiple of 100, although it would take many steps.
Q 14

Creative Chitti is a different kind of calculator. It has the following buttons: {tex}+1,+10,+100{/tex}, {tex}+1000,+10000,+100000{/tex} and +1000000. It always has multiple ways of doing things. "How so?", you might ask. To get the number 321, it presses +10 thirty two times and +1 once. Will it get 321? Alternatively, it can press +100 two times and +10 twelve times and +1 once.

Solution

First method:
Press {tex}+10,32{/tex} times {tex}\rightarrow 32 \times 10=320{/tex}
Press +1 once {tex}\rightarrow 1{/tex}
On Adding, 320 + 1 = 321
Yes, Chitti reaches 321 this way.
Second method:
Press {tex}+100,2{/tex} times {tex}\rightarrow 2 \times 100=200{/tex}
Press + 10, 12times {tex}\rightarrow 12 \times 10=120{/tex}
Press +1 once {tex}\rightarrow 1{/tex}
On adding, {tex}200+120+1=321{/tex}
Again, Chitti reaches 321.

Q 15

Two of the many different ways to get 5072 are shown below: These two ways can be expressed as:

  1. {tex} (50 \times 100)+(7 \times 10)+(2 \times 1)=5072 {/tex}
  2. {tex} (3 \times 1000)+(20 \times 100)+(72 \times 1)=5072 {/tex}

Find a different way to get 5072 and write an expression for the same.

Solution

{tex}(5 \times 1000)+(0 \times 100){/tex} {tex}+(7 \times 10)+(2 \times 1)=5072{/tex}

Q 16

Think and share situations where it is appropriate to

  1. round up and
  2. round down.
Solution
  1. Round Up:
    Buying food for a group or occasion. Buying materials so you don’t run short.
  2. Round Down:
    Shopkeeper saying lesser prices to attract buyers. Estimating fuel left to drive more carefully. Estimating remaining time to a meeting (e.g., saying 10 minutes left when it’s 12 minutes) to create urgency and avoid being late
Q 17

Think and share situations where it is appropriate to

  1. either rounding up or rounding down is okay and
  2. when exact numbers are needed.
Solution
  1. Either is Okay:
    Casual talks. Distance estimation between places.
    Telling academic results.
  2. Exact Numbers Needed:
    Handling money (bank balance, salary, tax).
    Doing science experiments or engineering work.
    Dialling emergency numbers (like 100 for the police)
Q 18

Write the five nearest neighbours for these numbers:

  1. 3,87,69,957
  2. 29,05,32,481
Solution
  1. 3,87,69,957
    Nearest thousand {tex}3,87,70,000{/tex}
    Nearest ten thousand {tex}3,87,70,000{/tex}
    Nearest lakh {tex}3,88,00,000{/tex}
    Nearest ten lakh {tex}3,90,00,000{/tex}
    Nearest crore {tex}4,00,00,000{/tex}
  2. 29,05,32,481
    Nearest thousand {tex}29,05,32,000{/tex}
    Nearest ten thousand {tex}29,05,30,000{/tex}
    Nearest lakh {tex}29,05,00,000{/tex}
    Nearest ten lakh {tex}29,10,00,000{/tex}
    Nearest crore {tex}29,00,00,000{/tex}
Q 19

I have a number for which all five nearest neighbours are 5,00,00,000. What could the number be? How many such numbers are there? (rounding off to the nearest 1000)

Solution

A number between 4,99,99,500 and 5,00,00,499 (inclusive of the first, exclusive of the second) rounds to 5,00,00,000 at all five levels. There are 1000 such numbers.

Q 20

From the information given in the table, answer the following questions by approximation:

  1. What is your general observation about this data? Share it with the class.
  2. What is an appropriate title for the above table?
  3. How much is the population of Pune in 2011? Approximately, by how much has it increased compared to 2001?
  4. Which city’s population increased the most between 2001 and 2011?
  5. Are there cities whose population has almost doubled? Which are they?
  6. By what number should we multiply Patna’s population to get a number/population close to that of Mumbai?
Rank City Population (2011) Population (2001)
1 Mumbai 1,24,42,373 1,19,78,450
2 New Delhi 1,10,07,835 98,79,172
3 Bengaluru 84,25,970 43,01,326
4 Hyderabad 68,09,970 36,37,483
5 Ahmedabad 55,70,585 35,20,085
6 Chennai 46,81,087 43,43,645
7 Kolkata 44,86,679 45,72,876
8 Surat 44,67,797 24,33,835
9 Vadodara 35,52,371 16,90,000
10 Pune 31,15,431 25,38,473
11 Jaipur 30,46,163 23,22,575
12 Lucknow 28,15,601 21,85,927
13 Kanpur 27,67,031 25,51,337
14 Nagpur 24,05,665 20,52,066
15 Indore 19,60,631 14,74,968
16 Thane 18,18,872 12,62,551
17 Bhopal 17,98,218 14,37,354
18 Visakhapatnam 17,28,128 13,45,938
19 Pimpri-Chinchwad 17,27,692 10,12,472
20 Patna 16,84,222 13,66,444
Solution
  1. Most cities have seen a significant rise in pollution from 2001 to 2011 with a few cities like Surat, Vadodara almost doubling their population. Some cities like Kolkata have shown very little growth.
  2. Population of major Indian cities in 2001 and 2011.
  3. Pune's population in {tex}2011=31,15,431{/tex}.
    Pune's population in {tex}2001=25,38,473{/tex}.
    Increase in population {tex}=3,115,431-2,538,473{/tex}
    {tex} = 576,958. {/tex}
    Approximately, the population increased by about 5.8 lakhs or nearly 6 lakhs.
  4. Population increase of some cities:
    Bengaluru: {tex}84,25,970-43,01,326=41,24,644{/tex}
    Hyderabad: {tex}68,09,970-36,37,483=31,72,487{/tex}
    Ahmedabad: {tex}55,70,585-35,20,085=20,50,500{/tex}
    Surat: {tex}44,67,797-24,33,835=20,33,962{/tex}
